query: X-ray showing free air in abdomen

retrieved_docs.title: Small Bowel Obstruction -- Evaluation -- Abdominal X-Rays
retrieved_docs.content: The initial imaging study of choice is usually a plain abdominal radiograph (x-ray). This study can reveal characteristic findings such as: Dilated loops of small bowel Air-fluid levels are visible on upright images, indicating proximal bowel distention. Absence of gas in the colon This finding suggests a complete obstruction, especially if the obstruction is distal to the small intestine. Step ladder pattern This pattern of air-fluid levels in the small intestine can indicate obstruction. Signs of perforation Free air under the diaphragm on an upright chest x-ray suggests perforation.

retrieved_docs.title: Ogilvie Syndrome(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Appropriate imaging is essential to the diagnosis and management of ACPO. Plain radiography, consisting of upright and supine abdominal films, is commonly performed but is limited in utility. Expected findings include varying degrees of colonic dilatation, normal haustral markings, and lack of signs of mechanical obstruction such as volvulus. Free air on acute abdominal series increases concern for perforation but does not rule perforation out. Perhaps the most important use of plain radiography is to measure the degree of initial colonic dilatation and for serial imaging during observation after diagnosis. CT scan with oral and intravenous (IV) contrast is the preferred modality for diagnosis. Rectal contrast is helpful but has been associated with iatrogenic perforation. Gastrografin or other water-soluble enteral contrast should be used. CT rules out anatomic or mechanical bowel obstruction as well as evaluates for more obscure causes of dilatation, such as retroperitoneal hematoma or abdominal abscess. CT scans can also show signs of ischemia, such as mucosal wall thickening, submucosal edema, or gas. Classically, ACPO on CT scan shows isolated dilatation of the cecum and ascending colon with a gradual transition zone or "cut off" at the splenic flexure. Suppose a CT scan is unavailable, and peritonitis is not appreciated during the examination. In that case, a contrast enema study can be performed, with a reported sensitivity of up to 96% in diagnosing ACPO. Lastly, diagnostic colonoscopy should be avoided in all patients with suspected ACPO, as gas insufflation is associated with an increased risk of perforation.

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Perforation -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic approach in patients with abdominal pain starts with plain films. The sensitivity of plain films for detecting extraluminal free air ranges from 50% to 70%. Ultrasound (US) has also been studied and shows excellent potential for identifying pneumoperitoneum. The most useful imaging modality is a CT scan that is highly sensitive and specific for free air. CT Findings for Perforation Pneumoperitoneum Mesenteric air Discontinuity of the hollow viscus wall Extraluminal enteric contrast Free intraabdominal fluid Extravasated intravenous contrast Bowel wall thickening or edema Mesenteric hematoma. In complex cases, one may need to perform a diagnostic laparoscopy to determine the cause and obtain and fluid for culture and biochemistry.

retrieved_docs.title: Small Bowel Obstruction -- Evaluation -- Abdominal X-Rays
retrieved_docs.content: Plain radiography has poor sensitivity, with rates ranging from 50% to 80%. While radiography can serve as an initial screening test for detecting air-fluid levels or free air in the abdomen, it does not provide sufficient imaging to identify the cause, extent, or complications of SBO. A small bowel diameter greater than 6 cm is concerning and indicates a severe obstruction that may pose a high risk for compromised viability.

retrieved_docs.title: Rigler Sign -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The Rigler sign is the second most common indicator of pneumoperitoneum, with the right upper quadrant subdiaphragmatic free air being the most common. Studies report that the prevalence of the Rigler sign is lower than that of right upper quadrant subdiaphragmatic free air (46% versus 32%). [11] Clear recognition of the Rigler sign typically requires a large quantity of free air, around 1000 mL. A pseudo-Rigler sign can occur when 2 loops of gas-distended bowel contact each other, thereby enhancing the radiopacity of the intestinal wall against the air-filled loops and potentially misleading the diagnosis of pneumoperitoneum. [12] If the Rigler sign is present, abdominal/pelvic CT scanning and surgical consultation are warranted in the appropriate clinical context. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Emphysematous Cholecystitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A computed tomography scan will confirm the presence of gas in the gallbladder wall or lumen and provide accurate information about the presence of air or fluid in the pericholecystic tissue, free air in the peritoneum, or rarely, retroperitoneum. The presence of air in the peritoneum or retroperitoneum represents a more severe form of emphysematous cholecystitis. A suggested classification according to radiological findings as progressive stages starting with the detection of air limited to the gallbladder lumen and progressing to findings of air in the gallbladder and the pericholecystic tissue as the most advanced stage. MRI can provide accurate information on the presence of intramural air and necrosis. Blood work will usually reveal a leucocytosis, and liver function tests may be normal or abnormal. Serum glucose levels are usually elevated.

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Perforation -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis usually confirmed by radiological imaging showing free intraperitoneal air. Another reported suggestive sign is the lack of an air-fluid level in the stomach in a horizontal beam view and a relative paucity of gas in the distal bowel.

retrieved_docs.title: Bowel Perforation -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging plays the central role in confirming the diagnosis. An upright chest x-ray or left lateral decubitus abdominal radiograph may reveal free intraperitoneal air beneath the diaphragm in 50% to 70% of cases, a classic but not universally present finding. The Rigler sign, which is the visualization of both sides of the bowel wall due to free intraperitoneal air, may also be seen. Abdominal radiographs may show dilated bowel loops, air-fluid levels, or loss of psoas margins in cases of retroperitoneal perforation. However, computed tomography (CT) with oral and intravenous contrast is the gold standard for diagnosis, as it can detect even small volumes of extraluminal air, extraluminal contrast leak, peritoneal fluid collections, and the underlying etiology, such as diverticulitis, tumor, or ischemia. CT also provides guidance for potential percutaneous drainage in cases of localized abscess. In rare instances when perforation is suspected but imaging is equivocal, diagnostic laparoscopy may be necessary for definitive evaluation. Together, laboratory data help stratify severity and systemic impact, while imaging defines the presence, location, and etiology of the perforation, guiding the urgency and type of intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Duodenal Perforation -- Evaluation -- Radiography
retrieved_docs.content: In acute upper abdominal pain, an urgent upright chest radiograph (CXR) is a basic essential test for duodenal perforation. Upright CXR shows free air below the diaphragm in 75% of patients. However, it can be normal, especially in patients who present early after symptom initiation. [50] Existing of free air on upright CXR in patients with abdominal symptoms establishes the diagnosis of PPU. In some patients with the abdomen, an X-ray may reveal the Rigler sign (air in the intestinal lumen and outside of the bowel wall), a football sign (a large volume of free gas appears in a large round black area), and gas surrounding the soft tissue structures such falciform ligament or liver edge. [51] [52] Normal upright X-rays do not rule out duodenal perforation, and in stable patients who are clinically suspicious of duodenal perforation, it is essential to evaluate by computed tomography scan. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Duodenal Perforation -- Evaluation -- Radiography
retrieved_docs.content: A non-contrast computed tomography scan is suitable to see free air below the anterior abdominal wall in patients with acute kidney injury. The diagnostic accuracy of PPU detection by computed tomography scan is about 98%. [53] Oral diatrizoate can help diagnose PPU when the computed tomography scan is unavailable and upright CXR does not show free air. However, the absence of leakage does not exclude PPU, as the perforation can seal off spontaneously. [54] A double-contrast computed tomography scan is the most valuable method for diagnosing duodenal perforation. [55] Computed tomography perforation features include duodenal wall thickening, extravasated oral contrast, extraluminal air, fat stranding, and periduodenal fluid collection. [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Abdomen -- Evaluation -- Children
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation of abdominal pain in children follows a similar approach to adults but requires specific considerations. Clinicians must exclude malrotation, a surgical emergency, in a child younger than 1 who presents with bilious vomiting. Children with abdominal pain and pharyngitis should undergo rapid strep testing. Healthcare professionals infrequently use CT scans of the abdomen in children due to the risks associated with radiation exposure. For children presenting with bilious vomiting, severe abdominal pain, abdominal distension, or signs of peritonitis, an abdominal radiograph is usually the initial diagnostic step. This imaging modality swiftly detects perforation through the presence of free air and identifies bowel obstruction by revealing air-fluid levels and distended bowel loops.

retrieved_docs.title: Diaphragm Injury(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned, diaphragm injuries secondary to blunt or penetrating traumas are frequently undiagnosed acutely. The absence of a hyperechoic, curvilinear line above of the liver or the spleen, detected by a FAST (Focal Assessment with Sonography for Trauma) examination during a trauma workup, may be the very first sign of a large diaphragm injury. More commonly, an upright plain film of the chest is often the best initial study, although it can miss up to 50% of all cases. A chest x-ray will frequently show elevated hemidiaphragm on the affected side, and visceral herniation can present as diffuse and loculated gas bubbles. This is often misdiagnosed as a pleural effusion or gas-forming empyema, and insufficient suspicion for diaphragmatic rupture can mislead the practitioner into placing a chest tube on the affected side. In severe cases, there may be herniation of other intraperitoneal structures into the affected side. A nasogastric tube can be placed to assist in making a diagnosis, and coiling of the NG tube into the chest on chest x-ray is diagnostic of a diaphragmatic rupture.  In less apparent cases, computed tomography (CT) of the chest and abdomen has higher sensitivity and specificity for diagnosing diaphragmatic rupture compared to a chest x-ray. Interruption of the diaphragm, herniation of abdominal viscera, constriction of abdominal viscera through the defect (the so-called "collar sign"), or free-floating diaphragmatic flaps are all signs concerning for diaphragm injuries. [2] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Gastric Volvulus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of a gastric volvulus can be suspected with a good history and physical exam; however, diagnosis can be difficult. The diagnosis of a gastric volvulus may be confirmed radiologically when patients are symptomatic. Plain chest x-ray films that are suspicious for a gastric volvulus demonstrate a spherical stomach or a double air-fluid level on upright chest films and the retrocardiac air-fluid level above the diaphragm on lateral chest film. Albas et al described 4 radiologic findings predictive for a gastric volvulus on an upper gastrointestinal (GI) contrast study by showing gastric air-fluid level above the diaphragm, a paucity of distal bowel gas, reversal of the relative position of the greater curvature of the stomach, and a downward pointing pylorus. A barium swallow may be helpful as an adjunct to chest x-ray. Computed tomography (CT) scans of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis can also be diagnostic. CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis can provide information about the nature of volvulus and other intraabdominal organs that may be involved, thus assisting pre-operative planning.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Radiographic Patient Positioning -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Considering radiographs are the most important and frequently used modality in clinical imaging, standardized patient positioning techniques are essential in evaluating any pathology of the body part being imaged. Frontal and lateral views of the chest allow a radiologist to evaluate for heart size, location of pneumonia, and the volume of pneumothorax, among other disease processes. [4] [5] Using both frontal and lateral views assists in accurately localizing processes, including foreign bodies, in the setting of aspiration or projectile injury. [6] Inappropriate patient rotation can degrade these images and the ability to evaluate subtle findings. The role of standard radiographs is somewhat limited in abdominal imaging. Erect and supine abdominal radiographs can be evaluated for free air, although erect radiographs are more sensitive in evaluating free air. [7] [8] Appropriate patient positioning can also affect the evaluation of small bowel obstruction, stool burden, portal venous gas, and abnormal calcifications. Appropriate positioning in mammography is even more critical in identifying the location of microcalcifications, a finding suspicious of malignancy. [9] Correct localization of microcalcifications on mammography is essential for planning percutaneous biopsy and localization for definitive surgical treatment of breast cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: X-ray Image Acquisition -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Although the role of standard radiographs is limited in abdominal imaging, abdominal radiographs can be evaluated for free air, small bowel obstruction, stool burden, and abnormal calcifications(such as in the setting of obstructing ureteroliths and gallstones). [13] Abdominal radiographs can also play a role in evaluating portal venous gas, pneumobilia, emphysematous cystitis, and pyelonephritis. Chest and abdominal radiographs are also commonly obtained to assess the placement and positioning of various support devices such as vascular catheters, chest tubes, enteric tubes, and surgical drains.

retrieved_docs.title: Subphrenic Abscess -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Leukocytosis with neutrophilia and an elevated sedimentation rate are common. A chest x-ray may show hemidiaphragm elevation, pleural effusion, and lung abnormalities. Blood cultures indicating polymicrobial bacteria are highly suggestive of a subphrenic abscess. Abdominal ultrasound is the gold standard for right-sided subphrenic abscesses, with high sensitivity for fluid collections. A computerized tomography (CT) scan seems better for detecting a left-sided subphrenic abscess. CT scan is the diagnostic imaging of any patient with suspected intraabdominal abscess. [7] Sagittal imaging on a CT scan is best for visualizing subdiaphragmatic abscesses. Abdominal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and an indium-111-labeled leukocyte scan may be helpful for hidden intraabdominal or subphrenic abscesses. MR enterography is the investigation of choice in patients with inflammatory bowel disease. Blood gas analysis may reveal respiratory alkalosis initially due to hyperventilation and may progress to metabolic acidosis if left untreated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium serves as the primary reference for the abbreviated term ct scan abdomen. The ct scan abdomen is clinically associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound imaging). Both imaging modalities are employed to evaluate abdominal pathology, and their clinical association reflects complementary roles in the diagnostic work‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure referred to as ct scan abdomen is clinically associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound imaging). In addition, ct scan abdomen is derived from the broader examination named Computerized tomography of chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium, indicating that abdominal CT imaging is linked both to ultrasound studies for clinical correlation and to contrast‑enhanced CT scans that encompass the chest and abdomen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 5 -- Community 2938
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis is classified as a CT scan abdomen, representing a focused imaging study of the abdominal region. CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis is also derived from the CT scan abdomen, indicating that it includes abdominal imaging as part of a broader examination that also covers the chest and pelvis. Both procedures share the core component of abdominal CT imaging, with the former being a dedicated abdominal and pelvic scan and the latter extending the coverage to include the thoracic cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2674
retrieved_docs.content: The CT scan of the abdomen (ct scan abdomen) is a specific type of combined CT of the chest and abdomen (CT of chest and abdomen), indicating that it falls under the broader category of thoraco‑abdominal computed tomography. Clinically, the ct scan abdomen is associated with abdominal ultrasound imaging (تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية), reflecting that both modalities are commonly employed together to evaluate abdominal pathology and complement each other in patient assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis and Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium are both classified as “Other CT scan,” indicating that each of these imaging procedures is considered an alternative or supplementary CT examination derived from a broader CT scan category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of chest, abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium is connected to the ct scan abdomen via an “other_mapped_from” relationship, indicating that the abdominal portion of the comprehensive contrast‑enhanced CT can be regarded as a distinct ct scan abdomen. The ct scan abdomen, in turn, is clinically associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound), highlighting that these two imaging modalities are often used together to assess abdominal structures and pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2150
retrieved_docs.content: Radiologic procedures on the chest and abdomen encompass a range of imaging techniques, and within this broader category the specific modality of radiography of the retroperitoneum is classified as a child procedure, representing a focused imaging approach to evaluate structures located behind the peritoneal cavity as part of comprehensive thoraco‑abdominal radiologic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis is a specific type of ct scan abdomen, and the more detailed procedure described as Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium also maps to the same ct scan abdomen concept. Both the CT of abdomen and pelvis and the contrast‑enhanced computerized tomography represent radiologic examinations that focus on the abdominal and pelvic regions. In clinical practice, ct scan abdomen is frequently associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound), indicating that these imaging modalities are often considered together when evaluating abdominal pathology, with each providing complementary diagnostic information.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 5 -- Community 2951
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis is classified as a specific type of ct scan abdomen. The procedure termed Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium is directly derived from, or primarily mapped from, the generic ct scan abdomen, indicating that the contrast‑enhanced study is a specialized form of the standard abdominal CT examination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2153
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis, CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis, and computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium are all classified under the broader category “Other CT scan,” indicating that each of these specific imaging protocols is mapped from or considered a subtype of the generic “Other CT scan” designation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2096
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis, CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis, and Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium are all specific imaging procedures that are mapped from the broader category “Other CT scan.” The “Other CT scan” category itself is a subcategory of the more general “Other x‑ray” classification. This hierarchical relationship groups these particular CT examinations under a generic CT label, which in turn is organized within the overall x‑ray imaging family, facilitating systematic categorization and management of radiologic studies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2114
retrieved_docs.content: Other diagnostic radiology and related techniques encompasses Soft tissue X-ray of abdomen as a subordinate modality; the Soft tissue X-ray of abdomen is a specific radiographic examination used to evaluate abdominal soft‑tissue structures and is classified within the broader category of other diagnostic radiology and related techniques. This hierarchical relationship aids in organizing imaging procedures for clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis is a specific type of ct scan abdomen, representing a focused abdominal imaging study. In addition, Computerized tomography of brain, chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium is also linked to the concept of a ct scan abdomen, indicating that abdominal CT imaging can be incorporated into broader multi‑region examinations that include the brain and chest and often employ intravenous contrast to enhance diagnostic detail. Together, these relationships illustrate that abdominal CT can appear as a standalone procedure or as part of an extensive, contrast‑enhanced CT protocol covering multiple body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: abdominal hernia (فتق البطن), and biliary disorders (اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ). Clinical findings such as an abdominal mass (كتلة في البَطْن) or acute abdomen (بَطْنٌ حادّ) are evaluated with the same imaging and procedural approaches. Understanding the interconnected anatomy of the abdomen and liver, the spectrum of hepatic diseases, their risk factors, and the potential toxicities of substances used in abdominal interventions is essential for safe procedural planning, accurate diagnosis, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: CT scan abdomen is a radiologic procedure that is interconnected with several related imaging terms and modalities. In an other_mapped_from relationship, the phrase “CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis” is linked to the term ct scan abdomen, indicating that the broader chest‑abdomen‑pelvis CT study can be considered a variant of an abdominal CT scan. In a primary_mapped_from relationship, “Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium” is directly associated with ct scan abdomen, highlighting the standard, contrast‑enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis as the primary representation of this imaging modality. Additionally, a clinically_associated_with relationship connects ct scan abdomen to تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (the Arabic term for abdominal ultrasound), suggesting that findings from an abdominal CT are often correlated with or complemented by ultrasound examination of the abdomen. Recognizing these synonym mappings and clinical associations helps clinicians select appropriate imaging strategies, interpret results across different modalities, and ensure comprehensive evaluation of abdominal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1301
retrieved_docs.content: CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis is a computed tomography examination that encompasses imaging of the abdominal region and is identified as a ct scan abdomen derived from other_mapped_from sources. Clinically, the ct scan abdomen is associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound imaging), indicating that these two imaging modalities are often considered together when evaluating abdominal pathology. This interrelationship underscores the complementary role of CT and ultrasound in providing comprehensive diagnostic information for patients.
